title: Why American data centers can't plug in
gist: The binding constraint on the US AI/data-center buildout is not power generation but grid interconnection: a first-come-first-served queue that now takes ~55 months and a transmission system built for rare peaks. The fixes are market-based—auction fast-track interconnection slots and pay loads/generators to be flexible (disconnect a few hours/year), with flexible data centers facing a 'large, fast, or firm—pick two' trilemma.

insights.claim: OpenAI and Softbank's Stargate data center campus in Abilene, Texas is expected to cost well over $40 billion and draw 1.2 gigawatts at peak load—as much as 313,000 median American family homes.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Single-site loads of >1GW reset the scale of grid planning and make interconnection—not chips—the gating factor for hyperscaler capex.
insights.quote: Stargate is expected to draw 1.2 gigawatts, as much as 313,000 median American family homes, at peak load.
insights.locator: One of the most expensive projects in history

insights.claim: A report by EpochAI and an energy research institute projected total AI computing power would reach 100 gigawatts worldwide in 2030 if the 2025 growth rate holds steady.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: A 100GW global AI compute target frames the total addressable demand for grid, generation, transmission and storage suppliers this decade.
insights.quote: total AI computing power would reach 100 gigawatts worldwide in 2030 if the 2025 growth rate stays steady

insights.claim: The CEOs of Nvidia, Meta and OpenAI all state that AI scaling is now energy-limited, with Jensen Huang saying 'every data center in the future will be power-limited.'
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: When the buyers of compute say power is the ceiling, the marginal dollar of edge shifts from GPU supply to power/interconnection enablers.
insights.quote: every data center in the future will be power-limited

insights.claim: Other AI-supply-chain facilities are also large loads: the biggest US battery manufacturing plants draw 115 megawatts and the first phase of TSMC's Arizona semiconductor plant will draw 200 megawatts.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Reshoring of chips and batteries compounds grid demand beyond data centers—watch interconnection timelines as a hidden constraint on fab and gigafactory ramps.
insights.quote: the first phase of TSMC’s Arizona semiconductor plant will draw 200 megawatts

insights.claim: Between 1990 and 2024 the price of electric motors fell 97.5%, power electronics 99.5%, embedded processors nearly 99.9%, and batteries 98.8%, while battery energy density per kilogram rose five-fold.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Sustained cost/performance curves underpin a multi-decade electrification demand wave—structural tailwind for grid, storage and power-electronics names.
insights.quote: Power electronics fell 99.5 percent in price, processors built into devices by nearly 99.9 percent, and batteries 98.8 percent.

insights.claim: Electrification is a long-term economy-wide shift independent of the AI boom, driven by electricity's instant, low-loss conversion to work—e.g. EVs cost roughly half as much to fuel and have half the lifetime maintenance cost of gas vehicles.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Even if AI demand disappoints, grid-interconnection reform stays a durable theme as transport, industry and heat electrify.
insights.quote: electric vehicles can cost half as much to fuel even though electricity is more expensive than gasoline

insights.claim: US costs from grid inefficiencies like congestion reached $11.5 billion in 2023, up 45% from the prior year, as operators are forced to run expensive local plants because transmission can't move cheaper power through bottlenecks.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Rapidly rising congestion costs are a direct margin and rate-base signal—bullish for transmission build-out and merchant generators behind constraints.
insights.quote: the additional costs incurred because of grid inefficiencies like congestion ran to $11.5 billion in 2023, an increase of 45 percent from the year before

insights.claim: ERCOT forecasts it won't have enough power to meet demand by summer 2028, and in 2025 PJM—the largest US grid—could not buy enough future generating capacity to meet projected demand.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Named near-term adequacy shortfalls in ERCOT and PJM mark concrete windows where capacity prices and merchant power economics inflect.
insights.quote: We need capacity – a lot of capacity.
insights.locator: The grid bottleneck / ERCOT PJM MISO

insights.claim: As of October 2025 there were 143.5 gigawatts of data centers seeking to connect across ERCOT—far above ERCOT's all-time peak demand of 85.9 gigawatts in August 2024.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Interconnection queue >1.6x historical peak demand quantifies the speculative-demand overhang and the scramble for scarce Texas grid slots.
insights.quote: there were 143.5 gigawatts of data centers seeking to connect as of October 2025

insights.claim: The last period of ≥5% annual US electricity-use growth was the 1950s-1970s (AC, refrigerators, washers); from 2005 to 2023 electricity use was almost completely flat, so the grid's institutions are tuned for stagnation, not growth.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: A regime change from two decades of flat demand explains why incumbent processes break—reform laggards face the steepest cost and reliability shocks.
insights.quote: From 2005 to 2023, electricity use was almost completely flat.
insights.locator: This system was designed for a different era

themes.theme: Generation mix is a distraction; markets self-balance

insights.claim: Which generation source 'wins' (gas, nuclear, solar) is the wrong debate—well-designed electricity markets settle the optimal balance automatically via marginal-cost dispatch and price signals.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Reframes the AI-power debate from picking technologies to fixing market/interconnection design—where the real policy alpha sits.
insights.quote: These simple fixes can give America a power grid fit for the future.

insights.claim: As solar was overbuilt in ERCOT (max output rose from 4 to 29.8 GW between Dec 2020 and Sep 2025), the value of 1pm power fell from 92.9% to 38.7% of the highest-priced hour, signaling the need for storage—ERCOT battery discharge then grew from near zero to 8.6 GW by Oct 2025.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Price-shape collapse from solar saturation is the demonstrated leading indicator that storage demand inflects—a repeatable trade across markets.
insights.quote: the value of power at 1pm relative to the highest-priced hour decreased from 92.9 percent to 38.7 percent
insights.locator: The market for electricity / solar price shape

insights.claim: The Inflation Reduction Act's $30/MWh tax credit for qualifying renewables lets wind plants offer power at negative prices and still profit, at times dragging whole-market electricity prices negative.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Policy-driven negative pricing distorts merchant economics for non-subsidized plants—key risk factor for thermal and storage revenue modeling.
insights.quote: making money from the tax credit even when they literally pay consumers to use them

insights.claim: In one typical ERCOT utility territory, regulated (grid-infrastructure) costs grew from 28% of the average residential bill in 2002 to 40% in 2025, meaning a rising share of retail prices is set by regulators, not markets.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Regulated cost creep on bills shapes political tolerance for transmission spending—watch rate-case dynamics as a constraint on build-out.
insights.quote: the portion of regulated costs on the average residential customer’s bill has grown from 28 percent in 2002 to 40 percent in 2025
insights.locator: The market for electricity / consumer prices

insights.claim: xAI's Memphis data center could initially draw only 8 megawatts from the grid, so it installed 422 megawatts of on-site gas turbines to run partially off-grid until transmission upgrades were completed.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Hyperscalers will self-supply hundreds of MW of behind-the-meter gas to beat the queue—near-term demand pull for turbines and distributed gen.
insights.quote: xAI installed 422 megawatts of on-site gas turbines

insights.claim: Because of the connection backlog, 62% of data centers are considering off-grid solutions and Google is even exploring a solar-powered data center in space.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Majority interest in off-grid power signals a large interim market for on-site generation/storage while interconnection reform lags.
insights.quote: 62 percent of data centers are considering off-grid solutions

themes.theme: The interconnection queue is the real bottleneck

insights.claim: The median power plant interconnection wait jumped from under 20 months in 2005 to 55 months by 2023, with median time from initial request to interconnection agreement at 34.2 months for 2023 agreements.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Quadrupled wait times are the single clearest quantifier of the bottleneck—projects that secure early queue position carry option value.
insights.quote: this had jumped to 55 months by 2023

insights.claim: The first-come-first-served, essentially-free queue mandated by state commissions and FERC produces mostly phantom projects: 72% of connection requests since 2000 were ultimately withdrawn.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: A 72% withdrawal rate exposes the queue as a tragedy-of-the-commons spam problem that pricing/auctions could fix—regulatory catalyst to watch.
insights.quote: 72 percent of requests to connect submitted since 2000 were ultimately withdrawn

insights.claim: Restudies driven by upstream withdrawals can explode costs—one 242-megawatt wind project's network-upgrade bill jumped from $33.5 million to $99 million after it was already operating.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Restudy risk makes interconnection costs un-budgetable, deterring quality projects—cost certainty reform would unlock stalled capacity.
insights.quote: a restudy increased a 242-megawatt wind project’s network upgrade costs from $33.5 million to $99 million

insights.claim: PJM's slow queue inflated its 2025 capacity auction to $14.7 billion from $2.2 billion a year earlier; the article estimates that interconnecting just 30% of stuck renewable projects would have cut the auction cost 63%.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: A 6.7x capacity-price spike tied directly to queue speed shows where reform converts into consumer savings—and where merchant capacity revenue spikes.
insights.quote: $14.7 billion at auction to make sure they’d have enough power to supply their customers, a huge jump from $2.2 billion the year before.

insights.claim: Existing reforms (higher deposits, land/duplication disclosure, cluster studies that share backbone-upgrade costs) help but fail to address the core flaw—the inflexible first-come-first-served queue—so backlogs keep growing.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Signals that incremental FERC reforms won't clear the logjam; only structural (auction) change does—calibrate expectations for queue normalization.
insights.quote: the backlog has only grown as they fail to address the fundamental irrationality of the interconnection process itself

insights.claim: The interconnection scramble is a tragedy of the commons that auctions can fix—analogous to tradable fishing quotas, which in the 1970s turned a destructive race-to-catch into an orderly, efficient market that lowered consumer prices.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: The ITQ analogy is the author's core mental model for grid reform—predicts auction-based fast-track slots as the likely policy endgame.
insights.quote: what had been a mad dash for fish became an orderly and efficient process
insights.locator: Tragedy of the commons / fishing quotas

insights.claim: Developer bids capture private information—permitting odds, endangered-bird nests, neighbor pushback—that administrative scoring mechanisms used in current fast-track proposals can never replicate.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Argues market bids beat bureaucratic scoring for slot allocation—a design distinction that determines which reform actually clears the queue.
insights.quote: administrative scoring mechanisms could never capture the subtleties that a developer’s bid would
insights.locator: Tragedy of the commons / auctions vs scoring

themes.theme: Flexibility, non-firm service and the data-center trilemma

insights.claim: Transmission is overbuilt for peaks that occur only a few hours a year: in 2024, 42% of ERCOT's capacity went unused for half the time, yet new plants must wait for those rarely-needed upgrades before connecting.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Massive idle headroom is the slack that flexible/non-firm connections can monetize without new wires—core to the fast-connection thesis.
insights.quote: In 2024, 42 percent of ERCOT’s capacity went unused for half the time.

insights.claim: ERCOT's default 'connect and manage' (energy-only / non-firm transmission) lets plants come online before upgrades if they turn off during peaks, and is a large part of why ERCOT moves projects through its queue faster than any other US grid.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: A working model exists—other RTOs adopting connect-and-manage would compress timelines and shift where data centers choose to site.
insights.quote: That’s a large part of why projects move through ERCOT’s queue faster than any other grid operator.

insights.claim: Despite being available in all organized US grids, 87% of interconnection requests by capacity still choose slower firm transmission service, because non-firm service forfeits availability/capacity payments.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Payment design, not technology, keeps developers in the slow lane—reforming capacity payments is the lever to scale fast connections.
insights.quote: 87 percent of interconnection requests by capacity opt for the slower firm transmission service

insights.claim: One study found 76 gigawatts of new loads could be added across most of the US if those loads agreed to disconnect for just 22 (non-consecutive) hours per year—short enough to be covered by batteries rather than thermal backup.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Quantifies that modest load flexibility unlocks ~76GW of headroom for free—the strongest case for flexible data centers and grid-scale storage.
insights.quote: 76 gigawatts’ worth of new loads could be added (across an area covering most of the US) if these new loads were willing to disconnect during just 22 hours per year.
insights.locator: Can’t we just use less energy? / 76 GW study

insights.claim: Grid-scale four-hour batteries can be built for $1,300 per kilowatt versus about $2,500 for combined-cycle gas turbines, making batteries the cheaper bridge for brief data-center disconnections.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: A ~2x cost edge for short-duration storage over peakers reshapes the economics of flexible-load backup—tailwind for grid-scale battery suppliers.
insights.quote: grid-scale, four-hour batteries can be built for $1,300 per kilowatt, as compared to around $2,500 for combined cycle gas turbines
insights.locator: The data center trilemma / NREL cost

insights.claim: Data centers face a trilemma—large, fast to come online, or firm grid service, but not all three—so large data centers that want to launch quickly will have to be flexible.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: A clean decision framework for siting/financing AI capacity: speed-to-power forces flexibility, favoring storage-equipped, connect-and-manage designs.
insights.quote: data centers can be large, they can come online quickly, or they can receive firm grid service, but not all three

insights.claim: Most electricity users are indifferent to real-time generation cost because of fixed rates—a dryer load whose wholesale cost ranges from -$0.12 to $20 always bills at about $0.64—so flat retail pricing causes irrational consumption during grid stress.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Flat-rate insulation blocks demand response; spread of time-of-use/dynamic pricing is the policy lever that would smooth peaks and lower average prices.
insights.quote: The wholesale cost of a clothes dryer load, which could consume four kilowatt-hours, could range from negative $0.12 to $20, but the retail customer always pays $0.64
insights.locator: Can’t we just use less energy? / fixed rates

insights.claim: Home batteries are the ultimate load-managing tool because they offset the whole home and don't need to pass through the interconnection queue (install in weeks-to-months), but matching one 2GW data center would require solar-plus-battery at nearly 175,000 households.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Distributed storage helps at the margin but can't scale to hyperscale loads—utility-scale solutions remain primary; residential storage is a niche tailwind.
insights.quote: would require installing solar and battery systems at nearly 175,000 households
insights.locator: Can’t we just use less energy? / home batteries

themes.theme: Future demand and the policy prescription

insights.claim: Beyond data centers, industrial electrification, hydrogen, desalination, heat pumps, EVs and even electric aircraft will all raise electricity demand, so the binding constraint of the 21st century is delivering power where it is needed, not generating it.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Positions transmission/interconnection—not generation—as the durable choke point across many demand vectors; the highest-leverage place to invest or reform.
insights.quote: the main constraint to supplying power-hungry infrastructure is not power generation – it is getting that power where it is needed

insights.claim: The author frames the constraint as a blessing because the fixes are 'simple': auction flexible fast-track interconnection slots and reward producers and large consumers willing to disconnect for a few peak hours per year.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Crystallizes the two concrete policy catalysts to track for de-bottlenecking US power—actionable signals that reform is materializing.
insights.quote: the solutions are simple
